name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زَادَتْهُ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنًا ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَزَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (١٢٤)
```

```english
Whenever a surah is sent down, there are some of them who say, ‘Which of you did it increase in faith?’ As for those who have faith, it increases them in faith, and they rejoice. (124)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَتْهُمْ رِجْسًا إِلَىٰ رِجْسِهِمْ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٢٥)
```

```english
But as for those in whose heart is a sickness, it only adds defilement to their defilement, and they die while they are faithless. (125)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِى كُلِّ عَامٍ مَّرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ لَا يَتُوبُونَ وَلَا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٢٦)
```

```english
Do they not see that they are tried once or twice every year? Yet they neither repent, nor do they take admonition. (126)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَّظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ هَلْ يَرَىٰكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ ثُمَّ ٱنصَرَفُوا۟ ۚ صَرَفَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُم بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (١٢٧)
```

```english
Whenever a surah is sent down they look at one another: ‘Is anybody observing you?’ Then they slip away. Allah has turned aside their hearts, for they are a people who do not understand. (127)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ مَا عَنِتُّمْ حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢٨)
```

```english
There has certainly come to you an apostle from among yourselves. Grievous to him is your distress; he has deep concern for you, and is most kind and merciful to the faithful. (128)
```

```arabic
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ ۖ وَهُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١٢٩)
```

```english
But if they turn their backs [on you], say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me. There is no god except Him. In Him I have put my trust and He is the Lord of the Great Throne.’ (129)
```

## (Commentary)

These are the verses that conclude the verses of *Bara’ah* (Repudiation). They mention the state of the believers (المؤمنين) and the hypocrites (المنافقين) upon witnessing the revelation of the Quranic chapters (السور). Through this, one of the signs (أمارات) of hypocrisy (النفاق) is established, by which the hypocrite is distinguished from the believer. This sign is their saying when the Quran is revealed: "Which of you has this increased in faith?" and their looking at one another, saying: "Does anyone see you?"

In these verses, Allah, the Exalted, describes His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a description that draws the hearts of the believers toward him, and He commands him to rely (التوكل) upon Him if they turn away from him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زادَتْهُ هذِهِ إِيماناً** **"Whenever a surah is sent down, there are some of them who say, ‘Which of you did this increase in faith?’"** until the end of the two verses. The manner of the question in their saying: "Does anyone see you?" indicates that the questioner is not free from something [of doubt] in his heart. For this question is naturally the question of one who finds no trace in his heart from the revelation of the Quran. It is as if he believes that the hearts of others are like his own regarding what they receive; thus, he searches for anyone whose heart was affected by the revelation of the Quran. It is as if he perceives that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) claims the Quran rectifies every heart, whether it is prepared and disposed for rectification or not, while he does not believe this. Whenever a new chapter is recited to him and he finds in his heart neither humility (خشوع) before Allah nor an inclination or affection toward the Truth (الحق), his doubt increases. This prompts him to ask others present at the time of revelation about it, until he settles in his doubt and increases in the firmness of his hypocrisy (النفاق).

In short, the question is that of one whose heart is not free from hypocrisy.

Allah, the Glorified, has detailed the matter of hearts and distinguished between the hearts of the believers and those in whose hearts is a sickness (مرض). He said: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** **"As for those who have faith,"** and they are those whose hearts are free from hypocrisy and innocent of sickness, and they possess certainty (يقين) in their religion by the context of the contrast. **فَزادَتْهُمْ** **"it increases them"**—the revealed chapter— **إِيماناً** **"in faith."** For by illuminating the soil of the heart with the light of its guidance (هدايتها), it necessitates the intensification of the light of faith within it; this is an increase in quality (الكيف). Furthermore, by containing new insights and realities from the Quranic knowledge and Divine truths, and by spreading the light of faith in them over the heart, it necessitates the addition of new faith upon the previous faith; this is an increase in quantity (الكمية). The attribution of the increase in faith to the chapter is of the nature of attribution to apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرة). In any case, the chapter increases the believers in faith, whereby their breasts are expanded and their faces brighten with joy: **وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ** **"and they rejoice."**

**وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ** **"But as for those in whose hearts is a sickness,"** and they are the people of doubt and hypocrisy, **فَزادَتْهُمْ رِجْساً إِلَى رِجْسِهِمْ** **"it only adds defilement to their defilement,"** meaning a new misguidance (ضلال) added to their old misguidance. Allah, the Glorified, has named misguidance "defilement" (رجسا) in His saying: **وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً كَأَنَّما يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّماءِ كَذلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** **"and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing into the sky. Thus does Allah lay defilement on those who do not have faith"** (Al-An'am: 125). The contrast occurring between "those who have faith" and "those in whose hearts is a sickness" implies that the latter do not have sound faith in their hearts; rather, it is doubt or denial (الجحد), and in any case, it is faithlessness (الكفر). Therefore, He said: **وَماتُوا وَهُمْ كافِرُونَ** **"and they die while they are faithless."**

The verse indicates that a chapter of the Quran is never devoid of an effect upon the heart of one who hears it. If it is a sound heart (قلبا سليما), it increases it in faith, glad tidings, and joy. If it is a sick heart, it increases it in defilement and misguidance, similar to what is conveyed by His saying: **وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً** **"We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the believers, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss"** (Al-Isra: 82).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَلا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِي كُلِّ عامٍ مَرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ** **"Do they not see that they are tested every year once or twice?"** The interrogative is for affirmation (التقرير), meaning: What is the matter with them that they do not reflect or take heed, while they see that they are afflicted and tested (يمتحنون) every year once or twice, yet they disobey Allah and do not fulfill the requirements of the Divine trial (المحنة الإلهية), nor do they repent or take a reminder? Had they reflected on that, they would have become aware of their duty and realized that persisting in this state leads them to the accumulation of defilement upon defilement, permanent destruction, and eternal loss.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى بَعْضٍ هَلْ يَراكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ** **"Whenever a surah is sent down, they look at one another [as if to say], ‘Does anyone see you?’"** This is another characteristic of theirs: when a Quranic chapter is revealed—and they are inevitably present—they look at one another with the look of one who says, "Does anyone see you?" This is the speech of one who hears a discourse he cannot bear, which causes his breast to tighten, his color to change, and anxiety and agitation to appear on his face. He fears that he might be noticed and the secret he has concealed in his heart might be exposed. Thus, he looks at some of those to whom he has entrusted his secret and informed of his inner state, as if inquiring whether anyone perceives the anxiety and agitation within us.

So His saying: **نَظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى بَعْضٍ** "they look at one another" refers to some of the hypocrites. This is evidence that the pronoun in His saying in the previous verse: **فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ** "there are some of them who say" also refers to the hypocrites. His saying: **نَظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى بَعْضٍ** "they look at one another" means a look of anxiety and agitation, fearing the exposure of their affair and the tearing of their veil. His saying: **هَلْ يَراكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ** "Does anyone see you?" serves to explain the look; that is, they look at one another with the look of one who says: "Does anyone see you?" The word *min* (من) is for emphasis, and *ahad* (أحد) is the subject of *yarakum* (يـراكم).

His saying: **ثُمَّ انْصَرَفُوا صَرَفَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ** **"Then they leave. Allah has turned away their hearts because they are a people who do not understand."** The apparent context is that the meaning is: then they departed from the presence of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while Allah had turned their hearts away from comprehending the Divine verses and believing in them, because they are a people who do not understand truthful speech. Thus, the sentence is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية) according to what some allow.

It is also possible that His saying: **صَرَفَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ** "Allah has turned away their hearts" is an invocation (دعاء) from the Exalted against the hypocrites, and there are parallels for this in the Quran. An invocation from the Exalted against someone is a threat of evil for them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ ما عَنِتُّمْ حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ** **"There has certainly come to you an Apostle from among yourselves. Grievous to him is your distress; he has deep concern for you, and is most kind and merciful to the believers."** **Al-'Anat** (العنت) is harm and destruction. The *ma* (ما) in His saying **ما عَنِتُّمْ** is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), interpreted as "your distress" (عنتكم). The "Apostle" intended, as the context of the two verses testifies, is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He described him as being "from among themselves" (من أنفسهم), and the apparent meaning is that he is a human being like you and of your species, as there is no evidence to restrict the address to the Arabs or Quraysh specifically, especially considering the presence of men from Rome, Persia, and Abyssinia among the Muslims at the time of the address.

The meaning is: There has certainly come to you, O people, an Apostle from among yourselves. Among his attributes is that your harm or destruction is grievous to him, and that he is deeply concerned for all of you—whether believer or otherwise—and that he is especially kind and merciful to the believers among you. Therefore, it is incumbent upon you to obey his command because he is an Apostle who only speaks by the command of Allah, and obedience to him is obedience to Allah. You should find comfort in him and be drawn to him because he is from among yourselves, and you should respond to his call and listen to him as he advises you.

From here it becomes clear that the qualifications mentioned in the discourse from the attributes—namely His saying "an Apostle" (رسول), "from among yourselves" (من أنفسهم), "grievous to him is your distress" (عزيز عليه ما عنتم), etc.—are all aimed at emphasizing the recommendation to respond to him and accept his call. This is indicated by His saying in the following verse: **فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُلْ حَسْبِيَ اللهُ** **"But if they turn away, say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me...’"**

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُلْ حَسْبِيَ اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَهُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ** **"But if they turn away, say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me. There is no god but Him. In Him I have put my trust, and He is the Lord of the Great Throne.’"** Meaning: if they turn away from you and refuse to accept your call, then say: Allah is sufficient for me (حسبي الله); there is no god but Him—meaning He is my Sufficer (كافي), there is no god but Him.

So His saying: **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "There is no god but Him" serves as the justification for his detachment from [other] causes and his seeking refuge in his Lord. Thus, He is the Sufficer, and there is no sufficer besides Him because He is Allah, there is no god other than Him. It is also possible that the word of monotheism (التوحيد) was brought for glorification, similar to His saying: **وَقالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللهُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ** **"And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!"** (Al-Baqarah: 116).

His saying: **عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ** "In Him I have put my trust"—which carries the meaning of exclusivity (الحصر)—is an explanation that clarifies His saying: **حَسْبِيَ اللهُ** "Allah is sufficient for me," which indicates the meaning of trust (التوكل) by implication. It has been mentioned in some previous discussions that the meaning of trust (التوكل) is for the servant to take his Lord as a trustee (وكيلا) who stands in his own place and takes charge of managing his affairs. This means turning away from seeking means through the secondary causes he knows—which are inevitably partial causes and deficient causes—and seeking refuge in the True Cause (السبب الحقيقي) to which all causes ultimately return.

From here, the reason for concluding the speech with His saying: **وَهُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ** **"and He is the Lord of the Great Throne"** becomes apparent; meaning the Sovereignty and Authority by which He judges everything and manages every affair.

Allah, the Exalted, only said: **فَقُلْ حَسْبِيَ اللهُ** "say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me’" and did not say: "so rely upon Allah," in order to guide him to rely upon his Lord while remembering these truths that illuminate the reality of the meaning of trust (التوكل). The correct view is that a person should not trust in the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرة) he perceives—which are inevitably only some of the causes—but rather he should employ what he knows of them according to the nature Allah has placed in them, while trusting in his Lord and relying upon Him for the attainment of his desire and purpose.

In the verse, there is an unmistakable indication of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) extraordinary concern for the guidance of the people. For the Exalted commands him to rely upon his Lord in the matter he is concerned with, which is what the previous verse explained regarding his intense desire and concern for the guidance of the people and their attainment of felicity. So understand that.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu 'Amr al-Zubayri, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), in a long tradition in which he mentions the perfection and deficiency of faith (الإيمان), he said: I said: "I have understood the deficiency of faith and its perfection—so from where does its increase come?" He replied: "The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَإِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زادَتْهُ هذِهِ إِيماناً ـ فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَزادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ ـ وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَتْهُمْ رِجْساً إِلَى رِجْسِهِمْ** (Whenever a surah is sent down, there are some of them who say, ‘Which of you did this increase in faith?’ As for those who have faith, it increases them in faith, and they rejoice. But as for those in whose hearts is a sickness, it only adds defilement to their defilement). And He said: **نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ ـ إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ آمَنُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ وَزِدْناهُمْ هُدىً** (We relate to you their history with the truth. Indeed they were youths who believed in their Lord, and We enhanced them in guidance)."

"And if it were all one [level], with no increase or decrease, none of them would have merit over another, the blessings therein would be equal, people would be equal, and preference would be nullified. However, by the perfection of faith, the believers enter Paradise; by the increase in faith, the believers excel in degrees before Allah; and by the deficiency, the negligent enter the Fire."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Zurarah ibn A'yan, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding: **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَتْهُمْ رِجْساً إِلَى رِجْسِهِمْ** "But as for those in whose hearts is a sickness, it only adds defilement to their defilement," he said: "Doubt (شكا) added to their doubt."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying: **لَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ** "There has certainly come to you an Apostle from among yourselves": Abu Nu'aym narrated in *al-Dala'il* from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "My parents never met in fornication. Allah continued to transfer me from wholesome loins to pure wombs—purified and refined. Never did two branches diverge except that I was in the better of the two."

**I say:** Many narrations have been cited in this regard from men among the companions and others, such as al-'Abbas, Anas, Abu Hurayrah, Rabi'ah ibn al-Harith ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib, Ibn 'Umar, Ibn 'Abbas, 'Ali, Muhammad ibn 'Ali al-Baqir, and Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon them), and others, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In the same source, Ibn al-Daris narrated in *Fada'il al-Quran*, Ibn al-Anbari in *al-Masahif*, and Ibn Mardawayh from al-Hasan, that Ubayy ibn Ka'b used to say: "The most recent part of the Quran [to be revealed] from Allah—and in another wording, 'from the heaven'—are these two verses: **لَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ** until the end of the verse."

**I say:** The narration is reported through another path from Ubayy ibn Ka'b, and it is not free from conflict with the narration that will follow, as well as with the previous narrations regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا يَوْماً تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى اللهِ** "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah" (Al-Baqarah: 281), stating that it was the last verse revealed of the Quran.

Furthermore, the wording of the two verses does not suit their being the last of what was revealed of the Quran, unless it is an allusion to some events that occurred during the illness of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), such as the tradition of the inkwell and the paper.

In the same source, Ibn Ishaq, Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and Ibn Abi Dawud narrated from 'Abbad ibn 'Abdillah ibn al-Zubayr, who said: "Al-Harith ibn Khuzaymah brought these two verses from the end of *Bara’ah*: **لَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ** until His saying **وَهُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ** to 'Umar. 'Umar said: 'Who is with you on this?' He replied: 'I do not know, by Allah, except that I bear witness that I heard it from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), comprehended it, and memorized it.' 'Umar said: 'And I bear witness that I heard it from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Had it been three verses, I would have made it a chapter on its own. Look for a chapter of the Quran and attach them to it.' So they were attached to the end of *Bara’ah*."

**I say:** In another narration, 'Umar said to al-Harith: "I shall never ask you for evidence for it; thus was the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)." There are other traditions with this meaning. We shall provide a full discussion on the compilation of the Quran and related researches in the commentary of Surah al-Hijr, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

We had hoped to dedicate a separate discourse at the end of *Bara’ah* to research the state of the hypocrites (المنافقين) in Islam, extracting what the Quran explains regarding their affair, along with an analysis of their history and a clarification of the corruption and trials they sowed among the Muslims. However, the length of the commentary on the verses has prevented us from that, so we have deferred it to another suitable place. We ask Allah for success, for He is its Patron.

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
